title: Customer Overview & Segmentation
text:
Fintech platforms collect large volumes of customer data related to demographics, financial product usage, and digital engagement behaviour. Analysing these data can help fintech companies understand their customer base, identify behavioural patterns, and improve customer experience. This project develops a visual analytics dashboard for a Colombian fintech customer dataset. By integrating interactive visualisations and data analytics techniques, the system enables users to explore customer demographics, product adoption, and behavioural patterns, providing insights that support data-driven decision-making for fintech product and marketing teams.



The proposed fintech analytics dashboard is organised into three analytical modules that together form a coherent visual analytics workflow.The analytical process follows a progressive logic:
Customer Overview → Customer Engagement → Customer Retention & Value
This structure allows users to gradually explore the customer lifecycle, from understanding who the customers are to analysing how they interact with the platform and finally identifying high-value or high-risk customers.


The first module focuses on understanding the structure of the customer base. It analyses demographic characteristics, geographic distribution, acquisition channels, and financial product adoption patterns.
This module answers fundamental questions such as:
• Who are the customers using the platform?
• Which demographic groups dominate the user base?
• Which financial products are most commonly adopted?
These insights provide the baseline understanding of the fintech customer population.



The second module focuses on how customers interact with the platform. It analyses digital engagement indicators such as application usage, feature adoption, and transaction activity.
In addition, it examines customer experience metrics including satisfaction scores, Net Promoter Score (NPS), customer feedback, and support interactions.
This module helps identify behavioural patterns and service experience issues that influence customer satisfaction.



The third module focuses on customer retention and business value. It analyses churn probability, customer lifetime value (CLV), and transaction behaviour indicators.
The goal of this module is to identify:
• customers with high churn risk
• customers with high lifetime value
• high-value customers that may also be at risk
These insights help fintech companies design targeted retention and growth strategies.
Together, the three modules create a complete analytical workflow that supports data-driven decision-making for fintech product and marketing teams.




This module presents the Customer Overview and Segmentation analysis for a Colombian fintech platform.
Understanding the structure of the customer base is critical for fintech companies because customer demographics, product adoption, and financial behaviour are closely related to customer engagement and retention.
This project aims to develop an interactive visual analytics dashboard that helps business teams better understand customer behaviour and make data-driven decisions.

This module aims to answer the following questions:

Who are the customers using the fintech platform?
Where are the customers geographically located?
Which financial products are most commonly adopted?
How do customer demographics and product adoption vary across segments?
Are there distinct behavioural customer clusters?

The insights derived from this module provide the foundation for the subsequent modules focusing on customer engagement and churn risk analysis.

title: Customer Overview & Segmentation
text:
3.1 Convert Data Types
Categorical variables are converted to factors to ensure correct handling in statistical analysis and visualisation.

customer_data <- customer_data %>%
mutate(
gender = as.factor(gender),
customer_segment = as.factor(customer_segment),
acquisition_channel = as.factor(acquisition_channel),
income_bracket = as.factor(income_bracket),
city = as.factor(city))

title: Customer Overview & Segmentation
text:
3.2 Create Age Groups
Customers are grouped into meaningful age segments to facilitate demographic analysis.

customer_data <- customer_data %>%

mutate( age_group = cut(
         age,breaks = c(18,25,35,45,55,70),
             labels = c("18-25","26-35","36-45","46-55","55+")))

These age groups allow the analysis of customer segmentation patterns across different life stages.

title: Customer Overview & Segmentation

title: Customer Overview & Segmentation
text:
3.4 Create Active Products Variable
A derived variable named active_products is created to represent the total number of financial products owned by each customer.

customer_data <- customer_data %>%
mutate(
active_products =
savings_account +
credit_card +
personal_loan +
investment_account +
insurance_product
)

This variable provides a measure of customer financial engagement on the platform.

title: Customer Overview & Segmentation
section: 4.2 Customer Segment Distribution by Age Group
text:
4.2 Customer Segment Distribution by Age Group
This visualisation shows how customer segments are distributed across age groups using a stacked bar chart, revealing which segments dominate each life stage.


Show the code
age_segment <- customer_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(age_group)) %>%
  count(age_group, customer_segment) %>%
  group_by(age_group) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n))
p_age_seg <- ggplot(age_segment,
       aes(x = age_group,
           y = pct,
           fill = customer_segment)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity",
           position = "stack",
           colour = "#2C3E50",
           linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("inactive"   = "#95A5A6",
                               "occasional" = "#3498DB",
                               "power"      = "#E67E22",
                               "regular"    = "#2ECC71")) +
  labs(title = "Customer Segment Distribution by Age Group",
       x     = "Age Group",
       y     = "Proportion of Customers",
       fill  = "Customer Segment") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"))
ggplotly(p_age_seg, height = 450)






The stacked bar chart reveals how the composition of customer segments shifts across age groups, helping identify which life stages are dominated by active power users versus inactive or occasional customers.

title: Customer Overview & Segmentation
section: 4.3 Transaction Frequency by Age Group and Segment
text:
4.3 Transaction Frequency by Age Group and Segment
This boxplot compares transaction frequency across age groups and customer segments, helping identify whether engagement differences are driven by age or segment type.


Show the code
p_txfreq <- ggplot(customer_data,
       aes(x = age_group,
           y = transaction_frequency,
           fill = customer_segment)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7,
               position = position_dodge(0.8)) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("inactive"   = "#95A5A6",
                               "occasional" = "#3498DB",
                               "power"      = "#E67E22",
                               "regular"    = "#2ECC71")) +
  labs(title = "Transaction Frequency by Age Group and Segment",
       x     = "Age Group",
       y     = "Transaction Frequency (log scale)",
       fill  = "Customer Segment") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"))

ggplotly(p_txfreq, height = 450)






Customer engagement level (segment) appears to have a stronger influence on transaction behaviour than age alone. Power users consistently show higher transaction frequency across all age groups, while inactive users remain low regardless of age. The log scale adjusts for the heavily right-skewed distribution.

title: Customer Overview & Segmentation
text:
4.4 Customer Behaviour Analysis
The scatter plot helps identify behavioural patterns among customer segments, particularly high-value users who demonstrate both high transaction frequency and high transaction value.


Show the code
set.seed(123)
sample_data <- customer_data %>%
  sample_n(5000)
p_scatter <- ggplot(sample_data,
                    aes(x = transaction_frequency,
                        y = average_transaction_value / 1000,
                        color = customer_segment)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4,size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm",se = FALSE,color = "#2C3E50",linewidth = 1) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("inactive" = "#95A5A6",
                                "occasional" = "#3498DB",
                                "regular" = "#2ECC71",
                                "power" = "#E67E22")) +
  labs(title = "Customer Behaviour: Transaction Frequency vs Transaction Value",
       x = "Transaction Frequency (log scale)",
       y = "Average Transaction Value (Thousands)",
       color = "Customer Segment") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
        legend.position = "right")
ggplotly(p_scatter, height = 450)


`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'






The visualisation helps identify behavioural differences in spending patterns.
Customers with higher transaction frequency and higher transaction value
may represent high-value segments, while customers with lower activity may
indicate inactive or low-engagement users.

title: Customer Overview & Segmentation
section: 4.5 Financial Product Adoption by Customer Segment
text:
4.5 Financial Product Adoption by Customer Segment
This visualisation compares the adoption rates of different financial products across customer segments.


Show the code
product_segment <- customer_data %>%
  group_by(customer_segment) %>%
  summarise(
    savings = mean(savings_account),
    credit = mean(credit_card),
    investment = mean(investment_account),
    loan = mean(personal_loan),
    insurance = mean(insurance_product)
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(-customer_segment,
               names_to="product",
               values_to="adoption_rate")

p_product <- ggplot(product_segment,
       aes(
         x = product,
         y = adoption_rate,
         fill = customer_segment
       )) +

  geom_bar(stat = "identity",
position = "dodge",
color = "#2C3E50",   
linewidth = 0.3,
alpha = 0.9) +
scale_fill_manual(
  values = c(
    "inactive" = "#AAB7B8",
"occasional" = "#5DADE2",
"power" = "#F0B27A",
"regular" = "#7DCEA0"
  )
) +
  labs(
    title="Financial Product Adoption by Customer Segment",
    x="Financial Product",
    y="Adoption Rate",
    fill = "Customer Segment"
  ) +

  theme_minimal()
ggplotly(p_product)






This analysis highlights how different customer segments interact with the fintech platform’s financial products and reveals potential opportunities for cross-selling strategies.

title: Customer Overview & Segmentation
text:
4.6 Behavioural Patterns Across Cities
Customer behaviour indicators are aggregated by city to explore geographic differences in platform usage.


Show the code
city_behaviour <- customer_data %>%
group_by(city) %>%
summarise(transaction_frequency = mean(transaction_frequency, na.rm = TRUE),
avg_daily_transactions = mean(avg_daily_transactions, na.rm = TRUE),
average_transaction_value = mean(average_transaction_value, na.rm = TRUE),
app_logins_frequency = mean(app_logins_frequency, na.rm = TRUE),
feature_usage_diversity = mean(feature_usage_diversity, na.rm = TRUE),
credit_utilization_ratio = mean(credit_utilization_ratio, na.rm = TRUE),
satisfaction_score = mean(satisfaction_score, na.rm = TRUE),
nps_score = mean(nps_score, na.rm = TRUE),
customer_tenure = mean(customer_tenure, na.rm = TRUE),
churn_probability = mean(churn_probability, na.rm = TRUE))
city_matrix <- city_behaviour %>%
column_to_rownames("city") %>%
as.matrix()
city_scaled <- scale(city_matrix)
heatmaply(city_scaled,
colors = viridis(100),
Rowv = TRUE,
Colv = TRUE,
k_row = 4,
main = "Fintech Behaviour Patterns Across Colombian Cities",
xlab = "Customer Behaviour Indicators",
ylab = "Cities",
showticklabels = c(TRUE, TRUE),
dendrogram = "row")

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
text:
Fintech platforms generate large volumes of customer-level data related to product adoption, digital engagement, transaction behaviour, service experience, and retention outcomes. While customer structure and product ownership patterns are important starting points, fintech product and CRM teams also need to understand how customers actually interact with the platform and how they evaluate the service experience they receive.
This project develops a visual analytics dashboard for the COFINFAD Colombian fintech customer dataset. The proposed system is designed as a web-enabled Shiny application that supports interactive exploration and data-driven decision-making for business users. Rather than functioning as a collection of isolated charts, the dashboard is structured as a multi-module analytical system that moves progressively from customer overview to engagement and experience, and finally to retention and customer value.



This prototype focuses on Module 2: Customer Engagement & Experience. The objective of this module is to analyse how customers interact with the fintech platform and evaluate their service experience.
More specifically, the module examines four connected aspects of customer behaviour and experience:

engagement breadth, represented by feature usage diversity and its relationship with product ownership,
engagement intensity, represented by app login frequency across product ownership groups,
experience evaluation, represented by the relationship between satisfaction and NPS,
and service friction, represented by complaint topic distribution and support burden.

These perspectives allow the module to move from how customers use the platform, to how they evaluate the service experience, and finally to where operational friction emerges.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
text:
2.1 Overall Project Storyline
The proposed Shiny application follows a progressive analytical workflow:
Customer Overview → Customer Engagement & Experience → Customer Retention & Value
This structure allows users to move from understanding who the customers are, to examining how they use the platform and how they experience its services, and finally to identifying high-risk and high-value customer groups.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
section: 2.2 Role of Module 2 in the Larger System
text:
2.2 Role of Module 2 in the Larger System
Module 1 provides the structural foundation of the dashboard by analysing customer demographics, acquisition channels, and financial product adoption patterns. Module 3 focuses on customer retention and business value through churn analysis and predictive modelling of Customer Lifetime Value (CLV).
Module 2 sits between these two modules and acts as the behavioural and experience diagnostic layer. It extends Module 1 by examining whether product adoption is associated with broader platform usage and whether interaction patterns translate into customer advocacy. It also supports Module 3 by showing how customer satisfaction, complaints, and support burden may help explain downstream retention and value outcomes. In this way, Module 2 functions as the analytical bridge between customer structure and business outcomes.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
text:
3.1 Required R Packages
The following R packages are used in this prototype module. All of them are supported on CRAN.

pacman::p_load(
  tidyverse,
  ggplot2,
  plotly,
  scales,
  forcats,
  patchwork,
  DT,
  lubridate,
  shiny,
  readxl
)


tidyverse supports data import, wrangling, and transformation.
ggplot2 is used to generate the prototype visualisations.
plotly can be used to convert static plots into interactive views for the future Shiny application.
scales helps format percentages and axes.
forcats supports categorical variable handling.
patchwork is used to combine plots during prototyping if needed.
DT supports interactive tables.
lubridate is used for date parsing and transformation.
shiny will be used later to implement the final interactive module.

These packages support data cleaning, categorical handling, grouped summaries, and the construction of prototype visualisations for engagement, experience, and service friction analysis. Some packages such as plotly, DT, and patchwork are included to support potential interactive extension, tabular display, and layout composition in the future Shiny implementation.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
text:
3.3 Variable Grouping for Module 2
For this module, the variables are grouped into three analytical categories.
Engagement variables - feature_usage_diversity - app_logins_frequency - bill_payment_user - auto_savings_enabled - product ownership variables such as savings_account, credit_card, personal_loan, investment_account, and insurance_product
Experience evaluation variables - satisfaction_score - nps_score - app_store_rating - base_satisfaction - tx_satisfaction - product_satisfaction
Service friction variables - complaint_topics - support_tickets_count - feedback_sentiment - resolved_tickets_ratio
These grouped variables support the analysis of how customers use the platform, how they evaluate their experience, and where service friction emerges.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
text:
3.4 Data Cleaning and Transformation
The code chunk below prepares the variables required for customer engagement and experience analysis.

customer_m2 <- customer_data %>%
  mutate(
    last_survey_date = lubridate::ymd(last_survey_date),

    customer_segment = factor(
      customer_segment,
      levels = c("inactive", "occasional", "regular", "power")
    ),

    acquisition_channel = as.factor(acquisition_channel),

    feedback_sentiment = factor(
      feedback_sentiment,
      levels = c("Negative", "Neutral", "Positive")
    ),

    complaint_topics = if_else(
      is.na(complaint_topics),
      "None",
      complaint_topics
    ),
    complaint_topics = as.factor(complaint_topics),

    support_ticket_bucket = case_when(
      support_tickets_count == 0 ~ "0",
      support_tickets_count == 1 ~ "1",
      support_tickets_count == 2 ~ "2",
      support_tickets_count >= 3 ~ "3+",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    support_ticket_bucket = factor(
      support_ticket_bucket,
      levels = c("0", "1", "2", "3+")
    ),

    feature_usage_bucket = case_when(
      feature_usage_diversity <= 1 ~ "0-1",
      feature_usage_diversity == 2 ~ "2",
      feature_usage_diversity == 3 ~ "3",
      feature_usage_diversity >= 4 ~ "4+",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    feature_usage_bucket = factor(
      feature_usage_bucket,
      levels = c("0-1", "2", "3", "4+")
    )
  )


project_teal <- c(
  "Not Own" = "#A7DDE3",
  "Own" = "#2D8F9B"
)

  "0" = "#DCEFF1",

  "Fees" = "#49A8B3",

The shared project-level cleaning logic also includes auditing duplicate or overlapping fields, validating range consistency, and checking cross-field consistency. In this module, special attention is given to complaint-related missing values because they directly affect the interpretation of service issue patterns. In addition, support ticket counts and feature usage diversity are grouped into diagnostic buckets to support clearer comparison in the prototype visualisations.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
section: 4.1 Average Feature Usage Diversity by Product Ownership
text:
4.1 Average Feature Usage Diversity by Product Ownership
A grouped bar chart is used to compare average feature usage diversity across product ownership conditions. This chart is suitable because product ownership is a binary status, and the objective is to compare average engagement breadth between customers who own and do not own each product type.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
section: 4.2 Average App Login Frequency by Product Ownership
text:
4.2 Average App Login Frequency by Product Ownership
A grouped bar chart is used to compare average app login frequency across product ownership conditions. This chart extends the engagement analysis from usage breadth to usage intensity and helps evaluate whether customers who own different financial products also log into the platform more frequently.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
text:
4.3 NPS Score by Satisfaction Score
A boxplot is used to compare NPS score distributions across satisfaction score levels. This is appropriate because nps_score is a continuous outcome variable, while satisfaction_score is a discrete rating category. The chart evaluates whether higher satisfaction translates into stronger customer advocacy.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
section: 4.4 NPS Score by Feature Usage Diversity Bucket
text:
4.4 NPS Score by Feature Usage Diversity Bucket
A boxplot is used to compare NPS score distributions across grouped feature usage levels. This chart provides a direct bridge between platform interaction and service experience by showing whether broader platform usage is associated with stronger customer advocacy.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
text:
4.5 Complaint Topics Distribution
A bar chart is used to visualise complaint topic frequencies because complaint topics are categorical issue types. By excluding customers without recorded complaints, the chart focuses on the actual composition of service issues.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
section: 4.6 NPS Score by Support Ticket Bucket
text:
4.6 NPS Score by Support Ticket Bucket
A boxplot is used to compare NPS score distributions across grouped support ticket levels. Grouping support burden into buckets improves interpretability and helps assess whether heavier service burden is associated with weaker customer advocacy.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
text:
5.1 Readability and Comparability
The prototype prioritises simple chart forms, clear axis labels, and limited visual clutter. Grouped bar charts are used where average comparisons are needed, while boxplots are used to compare distributions across discrete categories. This supports readability and makes the interpretation of behavioural and experience differences more straightforward.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
text:
5.2 Progressive Diagnostic Flow
The visualisations are organised into a progressive diagnostic sequence:

product ownership and platform usage,
usage and experience relationships,
and service friction.

This flow reflects the objective of the module by moving from how customers interact with the platform, to how they evaluate the service experience, and finally to where operational friction may emerge.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
text:
5.3 Chart-Type Suitability
Chart types are selected according to variable structure and analytical purpose. Grouped bar charts are used for average comparisons under binary product ownership conditions. Boxplots are used for grouped comparisons involving NPS distributions. Bar charts are used for complaint frequencies because they clearly display issue composition across categories.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
section: 5.4 Coordinated Views for Future Shiny Use
text:
5.4 Coordinated Views for Future Shiny Use
Although this prototype is not yet a full Shiny application, the visualisations are designed to support future coordinated filtering and drill-down. Users will later be able to explore how engagement and experience vary across product ownership groups, customer segments, acquisition channels, complaint types, and support burden through shared sidebar controls.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
text:
6.1 Proposed Inputs
The future Shiny module will expose the following input controls:

customer_segment
acquisition_channel
feedback_sentiment
complaint_topics
product ownership
support_ticket_bucket
feature_usage_bucket

These parameters allow users to investigate whether customer engagement, service experience, and friction vary across different customer subgroups.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
text:
6.2 Proposed Outputs
The module will display the following analytical outputs:

average feature usage diversity by product ownership
average app login frequency by product ownership
NPS score by satisfaction score
NPS score by feature usage diversity bucket
complaint topics distribution
NPS score by support ticket bucket
KPI summary cards
product-level engagement summary table
product-level login summary table

Together, these outputs help users understand how customers use the platform, how interaction connects to service experience, and where operational friction may influence customer advocacy.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
text:
6.3 Proposed UI Components and Layout
The proposed Shiny UI follows a sidebar–main panel layout.
Sidebar filters - selectInput() for customer segment - selectInput() for acquisition channel - selectInput() for feedback sentiment - selectInput() for complaint topics - checkboxGroupInput() for product ownership - selectInput() for support ticket bucket - selectInput() for feature usage bucket
Main panel - Top section: KPI cards for average satisfaction, average NPS, average support tickets, and average feature usage diversity - Middle section: product-related engagement views and experience evaluation views, including feature usage diversity, login frequency, and NPS by satisfaction - Bottom section: diagnostic views for interaction–advocacy linkage and service friction, including NPS by feature usage bucket, complaint composition, and NPS by support ticket bucket
This layout supports interactive exploration of customer engagement, customer advocacy, and service friction, while maintaining logical continuity with Module 1 and Module 3.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
text:
6.4 Early UI Storyboard Sketch
The figure below shows an early storyboard sketch for Module 2. It was used during the initial design stage to think through the overall sidebar–main panel layout, including the placement of filters and the arrangement of engagement and experience views.



Early UI storyboard sketch for Module 2


Although the specific charts in the final prototype were later refined based on the actual data patterns, this sketch remained useful as an initial reference for the module layout. In particular, it supported the decision to organise the module into a filter sidebar, a set of summary indicators at the top, and multiple diagnostic visual views in the main panel.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
section: 7.1 Average Feature Usage Diversity by Product Ownership
text:
7.1 Average Feature Usage Diversity by Product Ownership
The code chunk below is used to compare average feature usage diversity across product ownership groups.

product_engagement_summary <- customer_m2 %>%
  select(
    feature_usage_diversity,
    savings_account,
    credit_card,
    personal_loan,
    investment_account,
    insurance_product
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(
      savings_account,
      credit_card,
      personal_loan,
      investment_account,
      insurance_product
    ),
    names_to = "product_type",
    values_to = "owned"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    product_type = recode(
      product_type,
      savings_account = "Savings Account",
      credit_card = "Credit Card",
      personal_loan = "Personal Loan",
      investment_account = "Investment Account",
      insurance_product = "Insurance Product"
    ),
    ownership_status = if_else(owned, "Own", "Not Own")
  ) %>%
  group_by(product_type, ownership_status) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_feature_diversity = mean(feature_usage_diversity, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

p1 <- ggplot(
  product_engagement_summary,
  aes(
    x = product_type,
    y = avg_feature_diversity,
    fill = ownership_status
  )
) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = project_teal) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Feature Usage Diversity by Product Ownership",
    x = "Product Type",
    y = "Average Feature Usage Diversity",
    fill = "Ownership Status"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

p1








This visualisation shows that product ownership is strongly associated with broader platform usage. Across all five product types, customers who own the product consistently exhibit higher average feature usage diversity than those who do not. The largest differences appear for savings accounts, credit cards, investment accounts, and personal loans. This suggests that product adoption is not only a structural characteristic of the customer base, but also an important behavioural signal of deeper platform engagement.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
section: 7.2 Average App Login Frequency by Product Ownership
text:
7.2 Average App Login Frequency by Product Ownership
The code chunk below is used to compare average app login frequency across product ownership groups.

product_login_summary <- customer_m2 %>%
  select(
    app_logins_frequency,
    savings_account,
    credit_card,
    personal_loan,
    investment_account,
    insurance_product
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(
      savings_account,
      credit_card,
      personal_loan,
      investment_account,
      insurance_product
    ),
    names_to = "product_type",
    values_to = "owned"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    product_type = recode(
      product_type,
      savings_account = "Savings Account",
      credit_card = "Credit Card",
      personal_loan = "Personal Loan",
      investment_account = "Investment Account",
      insurance_product = "Insurance Product"
    ),
    ownership_status = if_else(owned, "Own", "Not Own")
  ) %>%
  group_by(product_type, ownership_status) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_logins = mean(app_logins_frequency, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

p2 <- ggplot(
  product_login_summary,
  aes(
    x = product_type,
    y = avg_logins,
    fill = ownership_status
  )
) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = project_teal) +
  labs(
    title = "Average App Login Frequency by Product Ownership",
    x = "Product Type",
    y = "Average App Login Frequency",
    fill = "Ownership Status"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

p2








This visualisation extends the engagement analysis from usage breadth to usage intensity. However, unlike feature usage diversity, average app login frequency appears almost identical between product owners and non-owners across all product categories. This suggests that login frequency is not a strong differentiator of customer engagement in the current dataset, and that breadth of feature use may be more informative than simple access frequency.

text:

  theme(legend.position = "none")

This visualisation shows a strong positive relationship between customer satisfaction and NPS. As satisfaction scores increase from 2 to 6, the distribution of NPS shifts upward substantially, moving from strongly negative values toward neutral or positive levels. This indicates that customer satisfaction is closely linked to customer advocacy in the current dataset and provides one of the clearest evaluations of service experience in this module.

The distributions suggest a modest upward tendency in NPS as feature usage breadth increases, but the separation across groups is not strong. This indicates that broader feature usage may be associated with slightly stronger advocacy, although it is not as powerful a driver as satisfaction itself.

title: Customer Engagement & Experience
text:
7.5 Complaint Topics Distribution (Excluding “None”)
The code chunk below is used to visualise complaint topic frequencies among customers with recorded complaints only.

complaint_summary_actual <- customer_m2 %>%
  filter(complaint_topics != "None") %>%
  count(complaint_topics, sort = TRUE)

p5 <- ggplot(
  complaint_summary_actual,
  aes(
    x = fct_reorder(complaint_topics, n),
    y = n,
    fill = complaint_topics
  )
) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = n),
    hjust = -0.15,
    size = 3.5
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = complaint_palette) +
  labs(
    title = "Complaint Topics Distribution (Excluding 'None')",
    x = "Complaint Topic",
    y = "Number of Customers"
  ) +
  expand_limits(y = max(complaint_summary_actual$n) + 250) +
  theme_minimal()

p5








In the data preparation process, missing complaint records were recoded as “None”. To understand the actual composition of service issues, this visualisation focuses only on customers with recorded complaints. The results show that complaint frequencies are relatively evenly distributed across customer service, fees, transaction issues, account security, and app performance. This suggests that service friction is spread across multiple dimensions of the platform rather than concentrated in one dominant issue category.

The KPI summary provides an overall view of engagement and service experience in the platform. Average satisfaction is 4.16, indicating a generally moderate-to-positive experience. title: Proposal
text:
The rapid growth of fintech platforms has generated rich customer-level data across demographics, product adoption, digital engagement, transaction behaviour, and service experience. However, many business and product teams still rely on static spreadsheets or disconnected dashboards that do not clearly show how these factors relate to retention risk and long-term customer value.
This project is built around one core analytical question:

How do customer structure, platform behaviour, and service experience relate to retention risk and customer lifetime value in a fintech platform?

Using the COFINFAD (Colombian Fintech Financial Analytics Dataset), this project develops a web-enabled visual analytics application for fintech product, CRM, and business teams.
The project follows a progressive analytical workflow:
Customer Structure → Customer Engagement & Experience → Customer Retention & Business Value
This storyline allows users to move from understanding who the customers are, to diagnosing how they engage with the platform and evaluate the service experience, and finally to identifying which customer groups matter most for retention and value creation.

title: Proposal
text:
3.2 Data Preparation Logic
The project uses a shared cleaning framework, followed by module-specific transformations.

3.2.1 Shared data preparation steps

Remove duplicate or overlapping fields such as first_tx and first_transaction_date, and audit redundant transaction summary variables.
Handle missing values according to variable meaning, for example recoding missing complaint_topics as "None" where appropriate.
Convert date fields such as transaction dates and survey dates into proper Date format.
Standardise categorical levels for demographic, behavioural, and feedback-related variables.
Validate numeric ranges and perform consistency checks across related fields.



3.2.2 Module-specific transformations

Module 1 creates age groups, validates spatial coordinates, and constructs active_products.
Module 2 creates feature usage and support ticket buckets, and recodes complaint-related missing values.
Module 3 rescales customer lifetime value, creates age buckets, and prepares modelling datasets for training and testing.

title: Proposal

4.1 Module 1 — Customer Overview & Segmentation

4.1.1 Objective
Module 1 serves as the foundation layer of the system. It introduces the customer base through demographic structure, product adoption, behavioural differences, and geographic usage patterns.


4.1.2 Analytical Methods
The analytical methods used in Module 1 include:

Descriptive analytics for customer profiles and distributions

Grouped comparison analysis across age groups, customer segments, and product categories

Exploratory behavioural analysis using transaction frequency and transaction value comparisons

Geographic aggregation and clustering to reveal city-level behavioural patterns

Interactive tabular exploration for customer-level inspection

title: Proposal
text:
4.3 Module 3 — Retention Risk & Customer Value

4.3.1 Objective
Module 3 serves as the prioritisation and modelling layer of the system. It connects behavioural insight to retention and customer value outcomes through exploratory analysis and predictive modelling.


4.3.2 Analytical Methods
The analytical methods used in Module 3 include:

Exploratory risk and value analysis across customer groups

Geographic value analysis using city-level CLV aggregation

Baseline predictive modelling using Linear Regression

Nonlinear predictive modelling using Random Forest

Model evaluation through predicted-versus-actual comparison

Variable importance analysis for interpretation of model drivers

    Project Focus
    Built Around One Analytical Direction
    
      The goal is not simply to display customer metrics. The system is designed to move from understanding the customer base, to diagnosing how customers engage and evaluate the platform, and finally to prioritising retention and value outcomes.
    

    
      
        Why this matters for fintech teams
        Product and CRM teams need to answer connected questions, not isolated ones. A customer segment is only useful when it can be linked to platform behaviour. Behavioural patterns are only useful when they help explain advocacy, churn risk, or customer value.
        Using the COFINFAD dataset, this project turns customer-level data into a structured decision-support workflow for business users who need interpretable, interactive analysis rather than static reporting.
      

      
        What the system is built to support
        
          Identify who the customers are and how they differ across segments and locations.
          Diagnose which usage and experience indicators actually matter.
          Connect behavioural and service patterns to retention risk and customer lifetime value.
          Support business prioritisation through interactive exploration and predictive modelling.
        
      
    
  



  
    Storyline
    The Storyline Drives the Entire Website
    
      There is no separate storyboard page in the final website. The storyline is presented here as the main analytical path of the project and the logic that connects all three modules.
    

    
      
        Progressive analytical journey
        
          The system starts with customer structure, moves into behavioural and experience diagnosis, and ends with retention and customer value analysis. Each stage prepares the context for the next one.
        
      

      
        
          1
          Customer Structure
          The first step establishes the analytical foundation. Users explore segment composition, product adoption, transaction behaviour, and geographic patterns to understand what the customer base looks like.
          
            Who are our customers?
            How are they distributed?
            What early behavioural patterns appear?
          
        

        
          2
          Behaviour & Experience Diagnosis
          The second step explains how customers interact with the platform and how they evaluate that experience. This layer distinguishes stronger signals from weaker ones and surfaces where friction emerges.
          
            Which engagement signals matter?
            How does satisfaction relate to advocacy?
            Where does service friction appear?
          
        

        
          3
          Retention Risk & Customer Value
          The final step moves from explanation to prioritisation. Users examine churn context, value concentration, and predictive CLV modelling to identify which customers matter most for retention and growth.
          
            Who is more valuable?
            Which groups need attention?
            How well can value be predicted?
          
        
      
    
  



  
    Modules
    Three Modules with Three Different Jobs
    
      The modules are not equal summaries of the same dataset. Each one plays a distinct role in the overall logic of the system: foundation, diagnosis, and prioritisation.
    

    
      
        MODULE 01
        Customer Overview & Segmentation
        Role: Foundation layer
        This module introduces the customer base through segment structure, product adoption, transaction behaviour, and city-level pattern discovery. It is the entry point for the rest of the system.
        
          Interactive customer explorer table
          Segment distribution across age groups
          Transaction behaviour by age and segment
          Geographic behavioural clustering
        
      

      
        MODULE 02
        Customer Engagement & Experience
        Role: Diagnostic bridge
        This module sits between structure and outcomes. It tests how product ownership relates to platform usage, how satisfaction translates into advocacy, and where complaints and support patterns reveal service friction.
        
          Feature usage breadth by product ownership
          Login frequency comparison
          NPS linked to satisfaction and usage
          Complaint and support burden analysis
        
      

      
        MODULE 03
        Retention Risk & Customer Value
        Role: Prioritisation and modelling layer
        This module extends the analysis to business outcomes. It combines churn context, geographic value patterns, and predictive modelling of customer lifetime value using Linear Regression and Random Forest.
        
          CLV distribution by segment
          Churn comparison across segments
          Geographic value analysis
          Predicted vs actual CLV evaluation
        
      
    
  



  
    Explore the Project
    How to Move Through the Site
    
      The website documents the logic of the system. The Shiny application is where interactive exploration happens. The pages below are arranged to make that transition clear.
    

    
      
        1
        Read the Proposal
        Start with the project framing, dataset scope, and system architecture before going into module-level detail.
        Open Proposal →
      

      
        2
        Review the Modules
        See how the three analytical layers work together and what each one contributes to the full workflow.
        Open Modules →
      

      
        3
        Launch the Shiny App
        Use the interactive dashboard to filter customer groups and inspect the main visual analytics views directly.
        Launch App →
      

      
        4
        Use the Poster and Guide
        Refer to the poster for a concise summary and the user guide for step-by-step usage support.
        Open User Guide →

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
Date: 11-Mar-2026 (Wednesday)
Time: 4:00pm – 5:00pm
Attendees: Ricardo Moreno, Li Gen, Huang Bingqian



Ricardo shared that he had explored several datasets that could potentially be used for the group project and introduced the COFINFAD (Colombian Fintech Financial Analytics Dataset) provided by the professor. Ricardo explained that the dataset contains information on customer demographics, financial attributes, product usage, and behavioural indicators, which can support multiple customer analytics tasks.
Li Gen reviewed the dataset structure and noted that the dataset contains two components: a customer dataset and a transaction dataset, with the customer dataset containing around 54 variables suitable for analysis. But the transaction dataset was very large, which may increase the difficulty of analysis and implementation. And also pointed out that the transaction dataset only covers one year of data, which limits its usefulness for long-term trend analysis.
Bingqian asked whether the transaction dataset might still be useful for analysing transaction behaviour over time. After discussion, the team decided to first focus on the customer dataset for the initial project development, since it was relatively clean and contained sufficient variables which allowed the team to concentrate on building meaningful customer insights rather than spending excessive time on heavy data processing since transaction type was not main focus.





Li Gen suggested that the team should review the data quality of the customer dataset, including checking missing values, validating variable ranges, and standardising categorical variables and duplicate records.
Ricardo thought that the location information in the dataset is currently difficult to use directly for analysis and suggested converting the location variables into latitude and longitude coordinates so that they can be used for geographic visualisations.
Bingqian agreed that transforming the location data would allow the team to create maps and spatial visualisations in the future dashboard. Also noted that date-related variables should be converted into proper date formats so that they can be used for further analysis.





Ricardo shared several possible analytical topics that the group could explore, including financial inclusion, customer lifetime value (CLV), churn analysis, customer satisfaction, cross-selling opportunities, and digital engagement behaviour.
Bingqian suggested that the project could be structured around customer analytics to create a clear and logical storyline, such as geographic adoption, user transaction behavior, customer segmentation and churn risk.
Li Gen proposed organising the analysis intothree analytical modules so that each member could focus on a different analytical component:
Module 1: Customer Overview / Segmentation
Understanding who the customers are and how they are distributed across demographic and product categories.
Module 2: Customer Engagement and Experience
Exploring how customers interact with the platform and analysing satisfaction and service issues.
Module 3: Retention Risk and Customer Value
Identifying high-value customers and customers with higher churn risk.
The team agreed that these modules form a progressive analytical workflow, starting from customer structure, moving to engagement behaviour, and ending with retention and business value insights.





Ricardo volunteered to handle the location-related data cleaning, including converting location values into latitude and longitude coordinates and work on Module 3: Retention Risk and Customer Value, focusing on churn probability and customer lifetime value.
Li Gen will work on Module 2: Customer Engagement and Experience and assist with checking missing values and duplicate records, focusing on analysing engagement behaviour and satisfaction indicators.
Bingqian will focus on Module 1: Customer Overview / Segmentation, analysing customer demographics, acquisition channels, and product adoption patterns.





Li Gen will prepare the first draft of the project proposal, outlining the overall storyline, dataset description, and module structure.
Ricardo will continue reviewing the dataset and start working on data preparation tasks, particularly checking data quality and preparing variables for analysis.
Bingqian will begin exploring possible visualisation ideas for the Customer Overview & Segmentation module and document the meeting minutes.
The team agreed to review the first draft proposal together and refine the analytical storyline before the submission deadline.
The meeting ended at 5:00pm.

title: Meeting Minutes
section: 2.2 Predictive Modelling and Dataset Performance Challenges
text:
2.2 Predictive Modelling and Dataset Performance Challenges

Ricardo raised a concern regarding the performance of predictive modelling in Module 3. When running the full customer dataset of 48,723 records with machine learning models such as Random Forest, the computation time was significantly long and caused the R session to become unresponsive in some cases.
The team discussed possible approaches to address this issue:


Reduce the dataset size by applying random sampling (e.g., 10,000 to 15,000 records) for model training and evaluation within the Shiny app, while using the full dataset for descriptive visualisations.

Switch to a lighter model such as logistic regression or decision tree for the Shiny prototype, and reserve more complex models for the report write-up only.

Pre-compute model outputs and cache the results as RDS files so that the Shiny app loads stored results rather than rerunning the model each time.

After discussion, the team reached a consensus that Ricardo will implement a combination of random sampling and result caching for the Shiny prototype. The full model results will be documented and discussed in the module write-up report rather than computed live in the app.
Bingqian noted a similar issue in Module 1, where the city-level behavioural heatmap using heatmaply took a noticeable amount of time to render when all cities were included. The team agreed to limit the heatmap to the top 30 cities by customer count to improve responsiveness.

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
2.3 GitHub Collaboration Issues and Resolution

The team encountered several issues during GitHub collaboration in the early development phase:
- Merge conflicts: Li Gen and Bingqian both made changes to the _quarto.yml configuration file simultaneously, resulting in merge conflicts when pushing to the main branch. The conflict was resolved by manually reviewing the differences and merging both sets of changes.
- Large file push failure: Bingqian encountered a push rejection because the .RDataTmp temporary file  was accidentally included in a commit, exceeding GitHub’s 100 MB file size limit. This was resolved by removing the file from the Git history using git filter-branch and adding .RDataTmp to the .gitignore file.
- Branch management: The team had initially been working directly on the main branch, which caused issues when multiple members pushed changes at the same time. Li Gen suggested that each member work on their own feature branch and create pull requests to merge into main, to reduce the risk of conflicts going forward.
The team agreed to adopt the following GitHub workflow going forward:


Each member works on a dedicated branch.

Pull requests are reviewed by at least one other team member before merging into main.

The .gitignore file will be updated to exclude temporary R files (.RData, .RDataTmp, .Rhistory) and large data files.

Li Gen will update the .gitignore and push the updated file to the main branch, and will also update the _quarto.yml render list to exclude the module_retention.rmarkdown legacy file to prevent rendering errors.

text:
3.2 User Guide Format and Structure

Bingqian shared a reference example from a prior group project that structured the User Guide as a slide-based document, where each slide contains an annotated screenshot of the interface with numbered callouts and a description box explaining the functions of each UI element.
The team discussed two possible formats for the User Guide:
- Option A — PowerPoint slide deck: Each slide covers one screen or tab. Screenshots are annotated with numbered labels and a side panel explains each element. This format is visually clear and easy for new users to follow.
- Option B — Quarto HTML document: A scrollable web page with embedded screenshots, step-by-step instructions, and in-line annotations. This format integrates directly with the project website.
After discussion, the team agreed to produce the User Guide as a slide document with screenshot annotations following the same style as the slide-based reference. This would ensure the User Guide is accessible online and consistent with the rest of the website.
The division of labour for the User Guide was agreed as follows: each member is responsible for writing the guide section corresponding to their own module. Bingqian will draft Module 1, Li Gen will draft Module 2, and Ricardo Moreno will draft Module 3. Bingqian will also write the Overview section given prior experience with the homepage design. title: User Guide
text:
User Guide
This page will explain how to use the Shiny application.

title: Retention Risk & Customer Value
text:
Fintech companies operate in highly competitive digital markets where customer engagement and retention are critical for long-term sustainability. Unlike traditional banks, fintech platforms rely heavily on digital interactions between users and financial services. Understanding how customers interact with these platforms is essential for identifying profitable customer segments and maintaining long-term customer relationships.



Any business requires significant marketing and operational costs when it wants to attract new customers. Retaining existing customers proves to be more cost-effective and provide more value for extended periods.
Customers who remain active on a fintech platform tend to perform more financial transactions, adopt multiple financial products, and contribute higher customer lifetime value (CLV). In contrast, customers who show declining engagement or dissatisfaction may be more likely to discontinue their use of the platform.
Identifying customers with high churn risk allows fintech companies to implement specific strategies for retaining those customers. Organizations also need to track which customers produce the most value because this information allows them to distribute resources toward their most profitable customer groups.



The project objective is to develop a visual analytics application using R and Shiny for the interactive exploration of fintech customer data.
This tools would allow users to dynamically filter data, explore patterns across multiple dimensions, and find relationships between behavioral indicators and financial outcomes.
It is intended to help analysts compare churn risk across customer groups, examine engagement and profitability, and identify customers at risk of leaving the platform.



This module adopts an Exploratory Modelling methodology. It focuses on exploring relationships between customer demographics, engagement behaviors, and financial performance indicators, with particular emphasis on predicting Customer Lifetime Value (CLV).
The analytical workflow begins with data preparation and transformation, where relevant variables are cleaned, standardized, and organized into meaningful groups such as demographic attributes, transactional behavior, engagement indicators, and financial outcomes. This step ensures that the dataset is suitable for both visual exploration and predictive modelling.
Following data preparation, exploratory modelling techniques are applied to investigate patterns and relationships between variables. Initial visualizations are used to explore churn probability across customer segments, allowing the identification of groups that may be at higher risk of leaving the platform. While churn exploration provides valuable contextual insights, the primary analytical focus of this module is the development of a predictive model for Customer Lifetime Value (CLV).
A machine learning predictive modelling approach is implemented to estimate CLV using behavioral, and transactional variables. This model supports the identification of key drivers of customer value and enables comparison between predicted and observed CLV values. The results are visualized through interactive charts that allow users to evaluate model performance and explore how customer characteristics influence financial value.
The final stage of the methodology integrates these analytical components into a dynamic Shiny dashboard, enabling users to interactively filter data by demographic variables such as gender, income, occupation, and age group. This interactive design supports progressive exploration, allowing users to examine churn patterns, engagement behaviors, and predicted customer value across different segments.
Overall, this methodology supports data driven decision making by combining exploratory visual analysis, churn exploration, and predictive modelling of customer lifetime value, providing insights into customer profitability and retention risk.

title: Retention Risk & Customer Value
text:
2.1. Evaluation of R Packages
For the development of this module and the visual analytics components, several R packages are required to support data manipulation, visualization, and formatting of analytical outputs.
The main packages used in this project include:







Package
Use




tidyverse
Used to support the overall data science workflow, including data transformation, cleaning, and preparation of the fintech dataset.


ggplot2
Creates the core visualizations in this module, including boxplots, bar charts, scatterplots, and geographic maps.


dplyr
Manipulates and summarizes the dataset, groups customers by segment or location and calculates metrics like average churn probability and average CLV.


scales
Improve visualization readability by formatting large financial values, percentages, and axis labels.


plotly
Adds interactivity to selected visualizations, enabling users to hover over elements.


randomForest
Develop an exploratory machine learning model that predicts CLV by capturing nonlinear relationships and interactions between variables.



All these packages are hosted and maintained on CRAN, ensuring compatibility with the R environment and allowing them to be installed easily.
To verify that these packages are available in CRAN, the following command can be used in R:

packages <- c("tidyverse","readxl","ggplot2","dplyr","knitr","scales","plotly","randomForest")

sapply(packages, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)

   tidyverse       readxl      ggplot2        dplyr        knitr       scales 
        TRUE         TRUE         TRUE         TRUE         TRUE         TRUE 
      plotly randomForest 
        TRUE         TRUE 


The code chunk below uses p_load() of pacman package to check if the packages are installed in the computer. If they are, then they will be launched into R.

pacman::p_load("tidyverse","readxl","ggplot2","dplyr","knitr","scales","plotly","randomForest")

title: Retention Risk & Customer Value
text:
2.2. Raw Dataset
The analysis uses the COFINFAD (Colombian Fintech Financial Analytics) dataset, which contains detailed information about fintech customers. The dataset includes demographic information, financial product usage, transaction activity, customer satisfaction indicators, and predicted churn probabilities.
The dataset contains approximately 48,000 customer records and more than 50 variables, providing a comprehensive view of customer behavior within a fintech platform.

fintech <- readxl::read_xlsx("../data/customer_data.xlsx")

Geographic Variables

title: Retention Risk & Customer Value
text:
3.1. CLV Distribution by Customer Segment
With this visualization we aim to explore how Customer Lifetime Value varies across different customer segments to identify patterns in customer profitability. By examining the distribution of CLV using a logarithmic scale (CLV values are highly skewed), the analysis highlights differences in financial contribution between segments.

ggplot(fintech_clean, 
       aes(x = customer_segment, 
           y = clv_millions,
           fill = customer_segment)) +
  
  geom_boxplot(
    alpha = 0.8,
    outlier.alpha = 0.3,
    width = 0.6
  ) +
  
  # Add mean points
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean,
    geom = "point",
    shape = 18,
    size = 3,
    color = "darkblue"
  ) +
  
  scale_fill_brewer(
    palette = "Blues",
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  
  scale_y_log10(
    labels = comma
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Customer Lifetime Value by Segment",
    subtitle = "Distribution shown on logarithmic scale",
    x = "Customer Segment",
    y = "Customer Lifetime Value (Millions, Log Scale)"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

Warning in scale_y_log10(labels = comma): log-10 transformation introduced infinite values.
log-10 transformation introduced infinite values.


Warning: Removed 1 row containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).


Warning: Removed 1 row containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_summary()`).









From this graph, it can be observed that the distribution of CLV varies significantly across customer segments. “Power” and “Regular” customers exhibit substantially higher median CLV values compared to “Occasional” and “Inactive” customers, indicating that higher levels of engagement are strongly associated with greater long-term financial contribution.
These findings highlight the importance of prioritizing higher engagement segments in customer retention strategies and support the development of predictive models aimed at identifying customers with strong value potential.

title: Retention Risk & Customer Value
text:
3.2. Churn Probability by Segment
We want to explore differences in churn probability across customer segments. By comparing segments, the analysis provides insight into which groups may require targeted retention strategies.

churn_by_segment <- fintech_clean %>%
  group_by(customer_segment) %>%
  summarise(avg_churn = mean(churn_probability, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(avg_churn)) %>%
  mutate(customer_segment = factor(customer_segment, levels = customer_segment))

ggplot(churn_by_segment, aes(x = customer_segment, y = avg_churn, fill = avg_churn)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = label_percent(accuracy = 0.1)(avg_churn)),
    vjust = -0.4,
    size = 4,
    fontface = "bold",
    color = "#08306b"
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "#c6dbef",
    high = "#08306b",
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = label_percent(),
    breaks = seq(0.30, 0.35, 0.01),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.08))
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0.30, 0.35)) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Churn Probability by Customer Segment",
    subtitle = "Segments ranked from highest to lowest churn risk",
    x = "Customer Segment",
    y = "Average Churn Probability"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  )








The results indicate that churn probability is relatively similar across segments, with only minor variations.
This suggests that churn risk alone may not strongly differentiate segments, highlighting the importance of combining churn indicators with behavioral and financial variables when developing the predictive CLV model.

title: Retention Risk & Customer Value
text:
3.3. Engagement Behavior vs Customer Value
Understanding the relationship between transaction frequency and Customer Lifetime Value is essential for identifying behavioral indicators that may serve as strong predictors in the development of the predictive model.

x_limit <- quantile(fintech_clean$transaction_frequency, 0.95)
y_limit <- quantile(fintech_clean$clv_millions, 0.95)

ggplot(fintech_clean, 
       aes(x = transaction_frequency, 
           y = clv_millions,
           color = customer_segment)) +
  
  geom_point(
    alpha = 0.4,
    size = 2
  ) +
  
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    se = FALSE,
    linewidth = 1,
    alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  
  scale_color_brewer(
    palette = "Blues"
  ) +
  
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = comma
  ) +
  
  coord_cartesian(
    xlim = c(0, x_limit),
    ylim = c(0, y_limit)
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Transaction Frequency vs Customer Lifetime Value",
    subtitle = "Relationship between customer engagement and financial value",
    x = "Transaction Frequency",
    y = "Customer Lifetime Value (Millions)",
    color = "Customer Segment"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'









The result of this scatter plot suggest that while higher transaction frequency is generally associated with higher customer value, the relationship is not strictly linear, as significant variability in CLV exists across all frequency levels. Additional behavioral and demographic variables will be needed to improve the accuracy and robustness of the predictive CLV model.

title: Retention Risk & Customer Value
text:
3.4. Geographic Distribution of Customer Value
By mapping CLV by city we can identify locations associated with higher value customers and highlight geographic areas that may contribute disproportionately to overall revenue.
In order to visualize the Top 5 cities with the highest average CLV, we need to follow the next steps:

First, we aggregate customers by city and calculates the average CLV and number of customers.


clv_location <- fintech_clean %>%
  group_by(city, department, latitude, longitude) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_clv = mean(clv_millions, na.rm = TRUE),
    customers = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(label = city)


We identify Identify the Top 5 cities by average CLV


top_cities <- clv_location %>%
  slice_max(avg_clv, n = 5)


We map it


colombia_map <- map_data("world", region = "Colombia")


map_plot <- ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(
    data = colombia_map,
    aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group),
    fill = "#f7fbff",
    color = "gray80",
    linewidth = 0.3
  ) +
  
  geom_point(
    data = clv_location,
    aes(
      x = longitude,
      y = latitude,
      color = avg_clv,
      size = customers,
      text = paste0(
        "<b>City:</b> ", city,
        "<br><b>Department:</b> ", department,
        "<br><b>Average CLV:</b> ", comma(round(avg_clv, 2)),
        "<br><b>Customers:</b> ", customers
      )
    ),
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  
  geom_text(
    data = top_cities,
    aes(x = longitude, y = latitude, label = label),
    size = 3,
    fontface = "bold",
    color = "#08306b",
    vjust = -0.8
  ) +
  
  scale_color_gradient(
    low = "#9ecae1",
    high = "#08306b",
    labels = comma
  ) +
  
  scale_size_continuous(
    range = c(2, 8)
  ) +
  
  coord_quickmap(
    xlim = c(-80, -67),
    ylim = c(-5, 13)
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Average Customer Lifetime Value by Location",
    subtitle = "Bubble size represents number of customers; labels show top 5 cities by average CLV",
    x = "Longitude",
    y = "Latitude",
    color = "Average CLV\n(Millions)",
    size = "Number of\nCustomers"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
    legend.position = "right",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank()
  )

Warning in geom_point(data = clv_location, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude, :
Ignoring unknown aesthetics: text

ggplotly(map_plot, tooltip = "text")





The results reveal that the largest cities in Colombia (Bogotá and Medellín), are not necessarily the locations with the highest average CLV. Instead, several smaller cities exhibit higher value customer concentrations.
This finding suggests that growth opportunities may exist outside major metropolitan areas, indicating that focusing marketing, retention, and expansion strategies on smaller cities could represent an effective approach for increasing profitability.
These geographic patterns also provide useful context for enhancing the predictive CLV model by considering demographic factors as potential predictors of customer value.

title: Retention Risk & Customer Value
text:
4.1. Linear Regression
Independent variables were selected based on their relevance to customer engagement and financial activity, including transaction frequency, number of active products, average transaction value, customer satisfaction score, and Net Promoter Score (NPS).

clv_model <- lm(
  clv_millions ~
    transaction_frequency +
    active_products +
    average_transaction_value +
    satisfaction_score +
    nps_score,
  data = fintech_clean
)

summary(clv_model)


Call:
lm(formula = clv_millions ~ transaction_frequency + active_products + 
    average_transaction_value + satisfaction_score + nps_score, 
    data = fintech_clean)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
 -457.3  -266.6  -189.4   -25.9 17452.2 

Coefficients:
                            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)                3.385e+01  8.285e+01   0.409    0.683    
transaction_frequency     -1.758e+00  2.104e+00  -0.835    0.404    
active_products            7.926e-01  3.043e+00   0.260    0.795    
average_transaction_value -3.189e-07  9.056e-07  -0.352    0.725    
satisfaction_score         7.189e+01  1.551e+01   4.634  3.6e-06 ***
nps_score                  1.909e-01  7.144e-01   0.267    0.789    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 791.4 on 48717 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.003074,  Adjusted R-squared:  0.002971 
F-statistic: 30.04 on 5 and 48717 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16


Once the variables were defined, the model was fitted using the cleaned dataset to quantify the relationship between engagement indicators and CLV.
The model served as a baseline predictive method, allowing for the interpretation of coefficient estimates and the assessment of how individual predictors contribute to customer value.

# Generate predictions
fintech_clean$predicted_clv <- predict(clv_model)

# Define zoom limits (95th percentile)
x_limit <- quantile(fintech_clean$predicted_clv, 0.95)
y_limit <- quantile(fintech_clean$clv_millions, 0.95)

ggplot(fintech_clean,
       aes(x = predicted_clv,
           y = clv_millions,
           color = clv_millions)) +
  
  geom_point(
    alpha = 0.4,
    size = 2
  ) +
  
  # Perfect prediction reference line
  geom_abline(
    slope = 1,
    intercept = 0,
    color = "#08306b",
    linewidth = 1,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  
  scale_color_gradient(
    low = "#9ecae1",
    high = "#08306b",
    labels = comma
  ) +
  
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = comma
  ) +
  
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = comma
  ) +
  
  coord_cartesian(
    xlim = c(0, x_limit),
    ylim = c(0, y_limit)
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs Actual Customer Lifetime Value",
    subtitle = "Dashed line represents perfect prediction",
    x = "Predicted CLV (Millions)",
    y = "Actual CLV (Millions)",
    color = "Actual CLV\n(Millions)"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
    legend.position = "right",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )








From the visualization we can see that this model is producing only a few repeated predicted values, while the actual CLV varies a lot.
Several of those predictors are likely discrete or heavily rounded:

active_products is a small integer
transaction_frequency may take only a limited set of values
satisfaction_score and nps_score are rounded scales

The Linear Regresion model combines these values, so many rows end up with the same or very similar prediction.
To address this limitation, a Random Forest model was developed to better capture non-linear relationships and interactions among variables, ultimately improving the accuracy and robustness of CLV predictions.

title: Retention Risk & Customer Value
text:
4.2. Random Forest
The model incorporates key behavioral, financial, and demographic variables. These variables represent important indicators of customer engagement and financial contribution.

rf_model <- randomForest(
  clv_millions ~
    transaction_frequency +
    active_products +
    average_transaction_value +
    satisfaction_score +
    nps_score +
    customer_segment +
    age_bucket,
  data = fintech_clean,
  ntree = 100
)

For this model, categorical variables were converted into factor variables to ensure they were properly handled during model training.

rf_data <- fintech_clean %>%
  select(
    clv_millions,
    transaction_frequency,
    active_products,
    average_transaction_value,
    satisfaction_score,
    nps_score,
    customer_segment,
    age_bucket,
    income_bracket,
    gender,
    occupation
  ) %>%
  na.omit()


rf_data <- rf_data %>%
  mutate(
    customer_segment = as.factor(customer_segment),
    age_bucket = as.factor(age_bucket),
    income_bracket = as.factor(income_bracket),
    gender = as.factor(gender),
    occupation = as.factor(occupation)
  )

The dataset was then divided into training and testing subsets to enable proper evaluation of the model’s predictive performance.

set.seed(123)

train_index <- sample(1:nrow(rf_data), 0.7 * nrow(rf_data))

train_data <- rf_data[train_index, ]
test_data  <- rf_data[-train_index, ]

The model was fitted using the training dataset and the selected behavioral and demographic predictor variables.

set.seed(123)

rf_model <- randomForest(
  clv_millions ~
    transaction_frequency +
    active_products +
    average_transaction_value +
    satisfaction_score +
    nps_score +
    customer_segment +
    age_bucket +
    income_bracket +
    gender +
    occupation,
  data = train_data,
  ntree = 300,
  importance = TRUE
)

The results of the Random Forest model were first examined to evaluate its structure and overall performance characteristics.

rf_summary <- data.frame(
  Metric = c(
    "Number of Trees",
    "Number of Variables Tried at Each Split (mtry)",
    "Mean of Squared Residuals",
    "Percentage Variance Explained"
  ),
  Value = c(
    rf_model$ntree,
    rf_model$mtry,
    round(rf_model$mse[rf_model$ntree], 4),
    round(rf_model$rsq[rf_model$ntree] * 100, 2)
  )
)

knitr::kable(
  rf_summary,
  caption = "Random Forest Model Summary"
)


Random Forest Model Summary


Metric
Value




Number of Trees
300.00


Number of Variables Tried at Each Split (mtry)
3.00


Mean of Squared Residuals
555729.64


Percentage Variance Explained
9.54





To better understand the contribution of each predictor to the Random Forest model, a variable importance analysis was conducted.

# Extract importance values from the fitted model
importance_matrix <- randomForest::importance(rf_model)

# Select available importance metric
importance_values <- if ("%IncMSE" %in% colnames(importance_matrix)) {
  importance_matrix[, "%IncMSE"]
} else if ("IncNodePurity" %in% colnames(importance_matrix)) {
  importance_matrix[, "IncNodePurity"]
} else {
  importance_matrix[, 1]
}

# Create dataframe
importance_df <- data.frame(
  Variable = rownames(importance_matrix),
  Importance = importance_values
)

# Convert to relative importance (%)
importance_df <- importance_df %>%
  mutate(
    Importance_Scaled = Importance / max(Importance)
  ) %>%
  arrange(Importance_Scaled) %>%
  mutate(
    Variable = factor(Variable, levels = Variable)
  )

# Plot variable importance
ggplot(
  importance_df,
  aes(
    x = Variable,
    y = Importance_Scaled,
    fill = Importance_Scaled
  )
) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "#c6dbef",
    high = "#08306b",
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format()
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Variable Importance in Random Forest Model",
    subtitle = "Predictors ranked by relative contribution to CLV prediction",
    x = "Predictor Variables",
    y = "Relative Importance (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )








Although “customer_segment” appears as the most important predictor in the Random Forest model, this result is expected because the variable already captures broad differences in customer engagement and loyalty. For this reason, the more analytically meaningful findings are the strong contributions of “satisfaction_score” and “nps_score”, which emerge as the next most influential predictors of CLV.
Having identified the key drivers of customer value, the next step involved applying the trained model to generate predictions and evaluate its performance on unseen data.
Predicted CLV values were generated using the trained Random Forest model and applied to the testing dataset.

test_data$predicted_clv <- predict(rf_model, newdata = test_data)

A scatter plot comparing predicted and actual CLV values was created to visually assess the performance of the Random Forest model.

x_limit <- quantile(test_data$predicted_clv, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE)
y_limit <- quantile(test_data$clv_millions, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE)

rf_plot <- ggplot(
  test_data,
  aes(
    x = predicted_clv,
    y = clv_millions,
    color = clv_millions,
    text = paste0(
      "Predicted CLV: ", comma(round(predicted_clv, 2)),
      "<br>Actual CLV: ", comma(round(clv_millions, 2))
    )
  )
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 2) +
  
  geom_abline(
    slope = 1,
    intercept = 0,
    color = "#08306b",
    linewidth = 1,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  
  scale_color_gradient(
    low = "#9ecae1",
    high = "#08306b",
    labels = comma
  ) +
  
  coord_cartesian(
    xlim = c(0, x_limit),
    ylim = c(0, y_limit)
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Random Forest: Predicted vs Actual Customer Lifetime Value",
    subtitle = "Dashed line represents perfect prediction",
    x = "Predicted CLV (Millions)",
    y = "Actual CLV (Millions)",
    color = "Actual CLV\n(Millions)"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

ggplotly(rf_plot, tooltip = "text")





Compared to the linear regression model, the Random Forest model produces a more continuous spread of predicted values and captures more variability in CLV.
This improvement occurs because Random Forest models can handle nonlinear relationships and interactions between variables, which are common in customer behavior data.
As a result, the predictions better reflect the complexity of customer engagement patterns and provide a more flexible and robust modelling approach than traditional linear regression.
